title: Taiwan Strait Tensions Update — Mar 27, 2026
summary: PLA maintained a moderate 6-sorties/day pattern on March 27 (4 crossing the median line), consistent with the post-NPC cadence, with a notable spike to 16 sorties on March 25 (13 crossing). Three gap-day event files were created covering March 20–21 and March 27, capturing the full week's PLA aerial and naval activity. A US envoy publicly pressed Taiwan's legislature to pass the stalled NT$1.25 trillion special defense budget, of which only 2 articles cleared committee by March 27; the opposition KMT and TPP back far smaller NT$380–400B alternatives. Taiwan's HIMARS letters of offer and acceptance were signed before the March 26 deadline, securing delivery queue placement. The Trump-Xi summit has been rescheduled to May 14–15, continuing to delay the $14B PAC-3/NASAMS arms package. TSMC Q4 2025 revenue hit $33.7B with Q1 2026 guidance up 38% YoY, and a new US-Taiwan trade deal cut tariffs to 15% in exchange for a $500B semiconductor manufacturing commitment.

title: Taiwan Conflict Update — Mar 26, 2026
summary: PLA aerial activity around Taiwan spiked to 16 sorties on March 25 (13 crossing the median line) following a quieter period of just 3 sorties on March 24, suggesting continued volatility in Chinese military signaling. Taiwan's HIMARS letter of offer and acceptance deadline expires today, with parliament having unanimously authorized signing on March 13 — covering $9 billion in US arms packages including HIMARS, TOWS, M109A7 howitzers, and Javelins. A $14 billion record arms package comprising PAC-3 and NASAMS interceptors remains ready for President Trump's approval but has been held back pending the now-postponed Trump-Xi Beijing summit, which Trump delayed 5–6 weeks due to the Iran conflict. Defense Minister Wellington Koo confirmed F-16V deliveries are expected to begin in September 2026, with the US establishing a special project team to accelerate the $8.28 billion backlog of delayed weapons shipments.

title: Taiwan Strait Update — Mar 22, 2026
summary: PLA military activity around Taiwan has fully resumed after a two-week lull during China's National People's Congress. On March 21, 26 PLA aircraft were detected operating around Taiwan with 16 crossing the median line, accompanied by 7 warships. Taiwan's domestic political gridlock continues, with the opposition-controlled legislature blocking defense spending increases. Washington's mixed signals on Taiwan policy are being closely monitored by Beijing. In December 2025, China conducted its most extensive military drills to date, simulating a complete island blockade.
